title: Reducible fibers and monodromy of polynomial maps
speaker: Danny Neftin
abstract: For a polynomial f∈ℚ[x], Hilbert's irreducibility theorem asserts that the fiber f−1(a) is irreducible over ℚ for all values a∈ℚ outside a "thin" set of exceptions Rf. The problem of describing Rf is closely related to determining the monodromy group of f, and has consequences to arithmetic dynamics, the Davenport-Lewis-Schinzel problem, and to the polynomial version of the question: "can you hear the shape of the drum?". We shall discuss recent progress on describing Rf and its consequences to the above topics.   Based on joint work with Joachim König

proof_stages.label: Reducible fibers and composition sources
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the reducible-value set Red(f) for a polynomial map P^1 -> P^1 and identifies the basic infinite sources of reducible fibers as value sets of left composition factors, with Ritt theory reducing these sources to a small list involving indecomposable factors, powers, and Chebyshev polynomials.

proof_stages.label: Hilbert-irreducibility reformulation
proof_stages.summary: The reducible-fiber problem is placed inside Hilbert irreducibility: for a polynomial over Q(t), Red(f) is a thin union of value sets of auxiliary curves arising from subfields of the splitting field, so the infinite part is governed by genus-zero and genus-one subcovers.

proof_stages.label: Dynamical and arboreal reinterpretation
proof_stages.summary: Iterating f turns reducible fibers into questions about when new reducibility appears in backward-orbit trees, equivalently about transitivity and largeness of Galois images in arboreal representations compared with iterated wreath-product automorphism groups.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of comparison problems to reducible fibers
proof_stages.summary: The Davenport–Lewis–Schinzel problem for reducibility of f(x)-g(y) and Kronecker/arithmetic equivalence for number fields and polynomial value sets are shown to feed into the same reducible-fiber framework, since common left factors or equal value sets force reducibility of the corresponding fiber differences.

proof_stages.label: Monodromy and the indecomposable case
proof_stages.summary: The monodromy group Gal(f(x)-t/Q(t)) is introduced, indecomposability is translated into primitivity of its permutation action, and classification results for primitive polynomial monodromy groups imply that, outside explicit exceptional lists, an indecomposable map has only the expected reducible values.

proof_stages.label: Large monodromy for decomposable non-solvable factors
proof_stages.summary: For decomposable polynomials whose indecomposable factors have non-solvable monodromy—equivalently excluding the power and Chebyshev solvable cases—the new result proves that Red(f) is just the value set of the first factor up to a finite set, apart from an explicit finite list of ramification exceptions, and that iterated monodromy remains very large by containing iterated wreath products of alternating groups.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and remaining solvable cases
proof_stages.summary: The large-monodromy theorem is applied to the Davenport–Lewis–Schinzel problem, Kronecker equivalence, and arithmetic dynamics, yielding only the expected trivial cases under the non-solvable-factor hypothesis and leaving the power/Chebyshev and rational-function analogues as the main remaining sources of interesting examples.

examples.name: Lattès maps as rational-function analogues
examples.note: In response to questions about why x^n and Chebyshev maps are special and what happens for rational functions, Lattès maps are mentioned as the additional dynamical source of exceptional composition behavior outside the polynomial setting.
examples.engagement_reason: Lattès maps are mentioned as rational-function analogues arising from elliptic-curve multiplication, but no specific Lattès map is constructed or analyzed.

passes.reason: Lattès maps form an infinite highly regular elliptic-quotient family invoked as rational-function analogues rather than as a refutation.

examples.name: Unicritical/cyclic polynomials in arithmetic dynamics
examples.note: The family referred to as cyclic/unicritical, essentially maps of the form x^d-c, illustrates the special cases where dynamical reducibility and arboreal open-image questions had previously been understood.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker names the unicritical/cyclic family such as x^d-c and cites known finiteness/open-image results, with only brief structural comments and no concrete computation on a member.

passes.reason: Unicritical/cyclic maps such as z^d+c coalesce the finite critical points into one minimally branched, root-of-unity symmetric family used as a tractable case.
Unicritical/cyclic maps have all finite critical points collapsed into one, giving minimal branching/maximal ramification and cyclic symmetry, and are cited as a tractable known family.
Unicritical/cyclic maps such as x^d-c have μ_d symmetry and a collapsed single finite critical point, the minimal critical structure, and are used as a special solved family.
The maps x^d-c are unicritical/monomial, with all finite criticality collapsed into one totally ramified point and cyclic fiber symmetry, and are cited as a known tractable family.
Unicritical/cyclic polynomials are cyclic covers with a single maximally ramified critical point, cited as standard known cases.
The maps x^d-c collapse the finite critical locus to one totally ramified point with cyclic deck symmetry, and are cited as a tractable special family.
Used as the tractable cyclic/unicritical family in dynamics, with the minimal one finite critical point and root-of-unity symmetry.
Unicritical/cyclic maps such as x^d+c have all finite critical points coalesced and minimal cyclic branching, and are used as a known solvable family rather than a refutation.
Unicritical/cyclic maps such as x^d-c have cyclic symmetry and a maximally collapsed single critical point, and are cited as special known cases rather than refutations.
Unicritical/cyclic polynomials form a highly regular family with one collapsed finite critical point, used as a known tractable case rather than to refute a claim.
Unicritical/cyclic polynomials are the special x^d+c/x^d-type family with root-of-unity symmetry and a single collapsed, totally ramified finite critical point, used as a tractable known case.

Unicritical/cyclic polynomials such as x^d+c are a systematic highly ramified family with the minimal finite critical structure and cyclic cover symmetry, used as known tractable cases rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: “Can you hear the shape of a drum?” analogy
examples.note: The spectral-geometry question about manifolds with the same spectrum or zeta function is used as an analogy for arithmetic/Kronecker equivalence of number fields and polynomial maps.
examples.engagement_reason: The drum question is invoked as an analogy motivating arithmetic/chronicle equivalence, but no drum or spectral example is analyzed.

passes.reason: The drum analogy invokes isospectral non-isometric objects as the standard failure of spectral data determining shape.

examples.name: Davenport pair x^8 and 16x^8
examples.note: The pair x^8 and 16x^8 is cited as the known nontrivial polynomial-equivalence example, since 16 is an eighth power modulo almost every prime, illustrating the value-set-mod-p version of Kronecker equivalence.
examples.engagement_reason: The pair x^8 and 16x^8 is cited as the known Davenport example and its value-set property is stated, without working through the modular verification.

passes.reason: The pair x^8 and 16x^8 is built from maximally ramified cyclic monomials and serves as a nontrivial Davenport pair against naive rigidity of value sets.
The monomial Davenport pair has collapsed critical structure, minimal branching and μ8 symmetry, and is cited as a nontrivial equal-value-set pair refuting naive rigidity.
The pair x^8 and 16x^8 consists of monomials with cyclic symmetry and minimal/collapsed branch structure, presented as a known polynomial-equivalent example rather than a refutation.
The monomial pair has totally ramified cyclic structure, and it is used as the standard Wang/Davenport local-global failure for value-set equivalence.
The monomial pair is highly cyclic and fully ramified, and is used as a nontrivial same-value-set pair refuting naive rigidity.
The monomial pair has collapsed one-critical cyclic structure and gives a nontrivial Davenport pair showing local value-set equivalence need not be globally trivial.
A nontrivial same-value-set mod-p Davenport pair defeating naive rigidity, with monomial power maps having minimal critical structure and μ8 symmetry.
The pair x^8 and 16x^8 gives a nontrivial same-mod-p-value-set Davenport pair, with monomial cyclic fully ramified structure.
